explicit_text: My name is William Lee Frazier, but most people call me Bill Frazier. I've been involved with the Daniel Boone Festival since 1983 and served as its president in 1991 and 1992. I'm from Barbourville, Kentucky, and have a deep family connection to the festival, as my father was the fourth president the year I was born and started the Indian representation there. Over the years, I've taken charge of the Indians at the festival, continuing a multi-generational tradition of the Fraziers caring for that aspect.
implicit_text: My name is William Lee Frazier, but most people call me Bill Frazier. I've been involved with the annual festival celebrating the famous frontiersman since 1983 and served as its president in 1991 and 1992. I'm from the town where the festival is held, and have a deep family connection to the event, as my father was the fourth president the year I was born and started the Native American representation there. Over the years, I've taken charge of the Native American groups at the festival, continuing a multi-generational tradition of the Fraziers caring for that aspect.

explicit_text: The Daniel Boone Festival has been a central part of my life since 1983 when I joined and helped revitalize it along with members of the Junior Chamber of Commerce. Back then, the festival was struggling financially and had only eight members, but we managed to save it from being sold to other counties. The festival serves as a homecoming event for Barbourville and Knox County, bringing together multi-generational families and people who have moved away, especially after the Second World War. It features Indian representation, crafts, booths, and community gatherings, making it a vital tradition for the area.

The festival serves as a homecoming event for the town and surrounding county, bringing together multi-generational families and people who have moved away, especially after the Second World War. It features Native American representation, crafts, booths, and community gatherings, making it a vital tradition for the area.

explicit_text: I am from Barbourville, Kentucky, which is where the Daniel Boone Festival is held and where my family has deep roots. Barbourville has a strong community spirit, especially evident during the festival and times of flooding when neighbors come together to help each other. The town is situated along the Cumberland River, which has shaped much of our local life, including flood events and the building of a floodwall to protect the community. Barbourville is also a hub for Indian representation at the festival, with buses historically bringing groups here from various places.

explicit_text: I was involved with the Junior Chamber of Commerce, or Jaycees, which is an organization for young adults with an age limit around 35 or 40. When I and some others were nearing that age, we wanted to keep active and started meeting regularly, eventually connecting with the Daniel Boone Festival. I brought about a dozen Jaycees into the festival, which helped revive it from near collapse. The Jaycees and Daniel Boone Festival share similar community-focused goals, but the Jaycees hold their big event in October while Daniel Boone's main event is in February.

explicit_text: Rick Bird has been a key figure in the Indian representation at the Daniel Boone Festival for over 25 years. He first got involved as a child around 1964 and returned in the mid-1990s to help out again. I found him after trying to organize other groups, and he has been a steady presence ever since. Rick also crafts blowguns and bows from river cane, showcasing traditional skills that enrich the festival's cultural offerings.

explicit_text: I've worked closely with the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians, especially through efforts to bring them to the Daniel Boone Festival. About 15 years ago, I traveled to Oklahoma and Texas to arrange a treaty involving three Cherokee nations, including the Eastern Band, to support their participation and the harvesting of river cane. I've gotten to know their leaders well, including Chief Michell, who has visited the festival multiple times. Their involvement adds significant cultural depth to the event.

I've gotten to know their leaders well, including their chief, who has visited the festival multiple times. Their involvement adds significant cultural depth to the event.

explicit_text: Chief Michell is a leader of the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians whom I know quite well. We met recently to discuss his possible return to the Daniel Boone Festival, though scheduling conflicts with their own events sometimes make it difficult. He has visited the festival a couple of times before and represents an important connection between the Cherokee people and our community. His presence at the festival is always welcomed and valued.

explicit_text: Karl Bleyl was a PhD doctor who studied the Cherokee people and noticed the decline of river cane, which was vital for their traditional basket making. His work highlighted how the demand for wicker furniture had led to overharvesting of cane, leaving the Cherokee without materials for their crafts. This insight helped inspire the River Cane Treaty, which allowed the Cherokee to harvest cane again and support their livelihoods. Karl's research was important in preserving this cultural tradition.

explicit_text: The River Cane Treaty was an agreement I helped arrange about 15 years ago involving three Cherokee nations, including the Eastern Band, to allow them to harvest river cane in our area. This treaty is unique because, as Rick Bird says, it's the only treaty the white man has never broken. It supports the Cherokee's traditional basket making and other crafts by providing access to cane along the Cumberland River and its tributaries. The treaty has been a source of pride and cultural preservation for both the Cherokee and our community.

This treaty is unique because, as Rick Bird says, it's the only treaty the white man has never broken. It supports the Cherokee's traditional basket making and other crafts by providing access to cane along the Cumberland River and its tributaries. The treaty has been a source of pride and cultural preservation for both the Cherokee and our community.

explicit_text: The Cumberland River runs through Knox County and has played a big role in my life and the community. It starts near Harlan, flows through Pineville, Barbourville, and beyond, eventually reaching Nashville, Tennessee, before emptying into the Ohio River. The river has caused flooding issues over the years, including a major flood in 1977 that damaged Pineville but was mitigated here by our floodwall. While I don't fish, many locals have historically swum and fished in it, and it also provides the river cane used by the Cherokee for basket making.

explicit_text: I've lived my whole life in Knox County, Kentucky, where the Daniel Boone Festival is a major community event. Knox County is shaped by the Cumberland River and its tributaries, which bring both benefits and challenges like flooding. The festival serves as a homecoming for many families from the county, especially those who moved away after the Second World War to work in places like Detroit. Knox County's history and culture are deeply intertwined with the river, the festival, and the traditions of the local and Native American communities.

explicit_text: Pineville, Kentucky, is upriver from Barbourville and was heavily affected by flooding in 1977 when their floodwall broke and flooded downtown. That event contrasted with Barbourville, where our floodwall held strong, protecting the community. Pineville's experience shows how important flood control is along the Cumberland River, which flows through both towns. The two communities are connected by the river and share similar challenges related to flooding and river management.

explicit_text: Harlan, Kentucky, is near the source of the Cumberland River, which starts above Harlan and flows through several towns including Pineville and Barbourville. The area around Harlan has experienced heavy rains, such as one year in 1977 when twelve inches fell in about a day, causing flooding downstream. Harlan's weather and geography directly impact the river conditions and the communities along its path. It's an important part of the river's story and the region's environment.

explicit_text: The Army Corps of Engineers has been responsible for constructing and maintaining the floodwall that protects Barbourville from Cumberland River flooding. When the floodwall was built, my father was hired to place field rock on its backside for protection. The Corps' work has been vital in safeguarding our community, especially during major flood events like in 1977. Their involvement shows the importance of federal support in local flood control efforts.

explicit_text: The Boone Trace is a historic route intersecting with the Wilderness Road near Barbourville. Steve Valentine, along with Mike Mills and another man, put up signs along the Boone Trace to mark its significance. There's a group dedicated to preserving the Boone Trace, and Steve Valentine is a good contact if you want to learn more. The Boone Trace is part of the area's rich pioneer history connected to Daniel Boone and early settlement.

The Wilderness Road remains an important symbol of our local history and identity.

explicit_text: The Second World War had a big impact on our community, especially in Knox County, where many men served and later worked in the automotive industry. After the war, a large group of veterans moved to places like Detroit to build cars, but they would come home for the Daniel Boone Festival and other holidays. This made the festival a kind of homecoming event, bringing together families and friends who had been apart. The post-war era shaped the identity of our community and the importance of the festival as a reunion.
